data.title: Levi Strauss: Organic Growth And A Healthy Dividend
data.description:
As we move deeper into the calendar Q4 earnings season, the stock market is showing continued signs of volatility. Momentum in tech stocks seems to be breaking, with stalwarts like Microsoft (MSFT) losing ground, while traditional health insurance companies as well as large banks are also being hit.

Amid the volatility, I continue to emphasize a bias toward small and mid-cap stocks, particularly in beaten-down sectors. Investors have sold out of this space over the past year due to a combination of tariff fears plus weak consumer spending: but as Levi's (LEVI) just demonstrated in its Q4 update, the reality is actually much better than feared.

I last wrote a buy article on Levi in early January, when the stock was trading at $22 per share. Since then, the stock has underperformed the markets with a ~10% loss. However, I see absolutely no fundamental red flags in the way. Levi Strauss's sale of Dockers is allowing the company to focus on its core denim line, with organic sales growth trending healthily in the mid single digits. Amid a solid dividend and healthy earnings expansion, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Levi Strauss.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this name, here is what I view to be the core bull case for the company:

  • Overstated retail fears. Retail and apparel stocks have been hammered by an overstated fear that consumer spending is frail. While it's true that Levi's growth in the U.S. has stalled, overall organic sales growth in the mid single digits still showcases healthy traction for the Levi's brand, particularly for its Originals line. Sales growth in Europe and Asia are also outperforming the U.S., as the company makes inroads in market share in overseas markets.
  • Greater focus on core lineup. Levi's has sold off its Dockers workwear brand, while also winding down its lower-priced Denizen line (sold in Target stores). The company's focus on its core heritage Levi's products is yielding meaningful improvements in sales growth.
  • Adept tariff management. Despite headwinds on raw materials from tariffs, Levi's is still maintaining healthy gross margins in the low 60s, which indexes on the high end for consumer apparel companies.
  • Well-covered shareholder returns programs. Levi's yields just shy of 3% while also consistently buying back stock. The company's shareholder returns are more than covered by ongoing cash flow.

We note that the biggest appeal to investing in Levi's is a modest valuation, especially in an expensive stock market. As shown in the chart below, Levi's is guiding to organic growth of 4-5% y/y in FY26, which continues along the company's current growth trajectory despite supposed consumer spending headwinds. The company is also expecting flat y/y gross margins (despite a full year headwind from tariffs, versus only a partial year in FY25) and a slight expansion in adjusted EBIT margins.

Against the midpoint of the company's $1.40-$1.46 pro forma EPS guidance, Levi's currently trades at a 14.3x FY26 P/E ratio. For company with mid single digit organic revenue growth, expected margin expansion, and a healthy dividend, we think this remains a bargain worth buying.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Levi's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Levi's revenue on an overall basis grew 1% y/y to $1.77 billion, beating Street expectations of $1.71 billion (-2% y/y) by an impressive three-point margin. Also, we note that the company was held back by a tough compare versus the sale of its Dockers IP (in July of lsat year, impacting through the third quarter). The company is gradually winding down Dockers operations, expected to be fully extinguished by the end of current Q1. The company also faced a tough comp from a 53-week fiscal year in FY24, versus a standard 52-week calendar in FY25.

Organic growth excluding Dockers and the calendar shift, meanwhile, clocked in at a stronger 5% y/y companywide, roughly holding versus 6% organic growth in Q3. The snapshot below, meanwhile, showcases the company's sales by segment. The Americas are the laggard here, and this is where the recent concerns about slower spending come into play. Still, 2% organic growth here remains salvageable. Meanwhile, Europe is leading the pack with 10% y/y growth, while the company's nascent efforts in Beyond Yoga are driving 45% y/y growth.

We do find it a possible risk that Levi's is branching outside of its main denim here again, as secondary lines like Dockers and Denizen haven't worked out extremely well for Levi's in the past. Still, we note there remains tremendous market opportunity here as Lululemon (LULU) loses its crown and cedes share to newer players like Beyond Yoga.

On the gross margin front, Levi's maintained a sky-high gross margin at 60.8%, falling -100bps due to the impact of tariffs. Adjusted EBIT margins also fell -180bps y/y, but here again the company faced headwinds from the calendar shift and a 14-week quarter in the prior year.

On a full-year basis, we still find impressive that Levi's still grew adjusted EBITDA at 11% y/y to $925.0 million.

One final note on dividends: Levi's is continuing to pay out $0.14 in quarterly dividends, or $0.56 (~2.7% yield) annually, which is costing the company ~$55 million quarterly or ~$220 million annually. In FY25, the company generated $308.2 million of free cash flow (net of capex), indicating relatively secure 71% dividend coverage. Given the company's plans to grow organic sales in the mid single digits alongside EBIT margin expansion in FY26, we expect dividend overage ratios to improve further next year.

Key takeaways

Levi Strauss remains one of the most iconic denim brands in the world, and despite overblown concerns on a consumer slowdown, we find clear evidence that Levi's is finding success in penetrating into overseas markets, especially Europe. The stock offers a modest <15x P/E valuation, a healthy and well-covered ~2.7% dividend yield, and attractive organic growth trends. To me, this remains a great diversification play for 2026.

data.title: Sun Communities Upgraded As Mobile Home And RV Portfolio Grows After Exiting Marinas
data.description:
Sun Communities (SUI) is not a typical residential REIT I come across, since it invests in a portfolio of MH (mobile home), RV (recreational vehicle), and UK properties, and it originally got my attention last summer when I recommended holding on to it. Since my June article, it is up almost +1%. Nothing amazing, however; it shows the stock has had market resilience.

Key factors driving my optimism last time were an investment-grade credit rating, along with signs of macro demand for manufactured homes; however, its profit margins made me hesitant to be overly bullish.

As the REIT is having its next earnings results in about a month on Feb. 26th, this is a great opportunity for a follow-up article to get ahead of that event. Thesis Summary

For my updated coverage this time, my thesis argues for an upgrade to a buy. What really drives home the case for this REIT is the supply/demand imbalance in the modular home niche, and further support comes from proven dividend growth, portfolio expansion to drive company growth, and some investment-grade credit scores. These factors overshadow some weaknesses, including a fickle technical chart, lumpy FFO growth, and some seasonality impacts to its business.

My buy rating agrees with today's Wall St. consensus view.

The worksheet below shows what categories were considered and reviewed, contributing holistically to the average score:

Actionable Insights

Macro & Sector Outlook

This REIT happens to focus heavily on a residential niche called modular homes, whether they be for rent or sale. In 2025, notable to mention is that JPMorgan pointed out the appeal of this niche, saying, "High construction costs and lengthy project timelines are major barriers to meeting affordable housing demand. Modular homes offer a solution by potentially reducing both timelines and costs, making them ideal for affordable housing projects."

I believe this puts the spotlight on REITs like this one, and to add some flavor to this story, here is a sample property from their portfolio to get a sense of what they do:

The other niche this REIT focuses on is RV/campground sites via their brand Sun Outdoors, and it is worth mentioning that another major financial site last year wrote that "a generation of post-COVID remote workers and downsizers have embraced recreational vehicle (RV) living - not just for weekend escapes, but as a new way of life. Over 87.9 million North Americans are campers, and nearly half of them travel by RV."

So, the above points set the stage for demand drivers in both the mobile/manufactured as well as RV/camping niches, driven both by economics and changing trends. However, another market factor that could continue driving upside to a REIT like this is a supply/demand imbalance.

Consider from the firm's own Dec 2025 investor presentation (pg 10) that data shows hardly any new supply being added in the "manufactured housing" niche, even though other property categories did have some supply growth:

Based on the evidence shown, I gave this stock a strong buy in this category and considered this macro environment of the key upside factors that could benefit this REIT in the long term.

Competitive Positioning

When it comes to SUI's competitive position in this niche, I looked for similar REITs with a heavy focus on manufactured homes, and I think two key peers to consider are Equity Lifestyle Properties (ELS) and UMH Properties (UMH), with all 3 REITs being in the $1B - $16B market cap range.

To get a historical perspective, over the last 10 years, SUI saw a +92.5% price return, far below that of the S&P 500 as a benchmark but beating both of its peers in that regard, as momentum data shows:

I believe that shows the market resilience of this stock over a longer period where it was able to prove itself.

However, from another angle, if considering return on equity/ROE for that same period, SUI actually fell behind this peer group with an ROE of -4.7%, while its peer Equity saw vastly better returns here:

Where I think SUI could show some added value in this competitive niche is its growth in the UK market. From its December investor deck (pg. 12), we can learn that it focuses on "irreplaceable coastal 'destination' locations close to London and other major cities," but also can benefit from "numerous barriers to entry, including strict regulations and scarcity of land."

So, considering the various points raised in this section, I gave the stock a neutral, or hold rating, having cautious optimism about its positioning.

Company Growth Factors

When it comes to growth in this REIT, I am interested in its portfolio net growth, and along those lines, we know from the Q3 results press release (pg. 2) that "in October 2025, a total of 11 MH properties and 3 Annual RV properties" were acquired, while cash was raised by disposing of marina properties and a few RV properties.

With the firm exiting its marinas business segment called Safe Harbor Marinas, which it sold to Blackstone, it seems the focus shift is further investment into its key segments such as MH and RV, which I already showed to have upside potential.

Acquiring new property and portfolio expansion, as I've said in my opinions on other REITs, can drive future revenue flows for the REIT, so I consider it an upside factor. In fact, it makes REITs one of the easiest businesses to understand, actually, because there can be a correlation between portfolio growth and future rental cash flows.

Its UK presence is also a growth factor outside its home market of the US, which I mentioned earlier.

Another point I would bring up is that this REIT upgraded its guidance for full FY25 revenue growth, particularly in the North American mobile home and RV segment, in the Q3 results press release, which I think should add confidence to those of us focusing on top-line growth potential:

Thus, a key focal point for the upcoming FY25 full-year results will be seeing if they actually could execute on that guidance, because I think a major revenue growth miss could add to the downside for this stock.

I think the macro and company growth factors should drive the tailwind needed, and so I called this stock a buy in this category.

Profitability Metrics

In this section, I'll focus on the metrics that matter to the profitability of REITs specifically and compare them among similar peers.

When it comes to a key metric for REITs like FFO growth per share, there is a mixed picture for this firm. For instance, the chart below shows that over 3 years the FFO growth has been very choppy and not a steady growth, and it seems that FFO spikes in the middle/late-middle part of the fiscal year and takes a dive in winter:

This would likely make sense for a REIT that has a considerable RV/camping segment, which attracts the outdoor recreational customer, but also branding itself as the brand for sunny living implies to me that there could be some seasonal impact to this business.

In terms of the core business efficiency, though, the EBITDA margin could be an interesting metric (since it ignores depreciation, a non-cash expense), and so with an EBITDA margin of around +41%, it puts SUI behind two key peers in this niche; however, it is not a major outlier, and the trend line seems to be sloping upward, which is a positive sign:

A related metric, the AFFO, which I think is also important as it can impact dividend coverage, also could have done better in the last 3 years, with a 3-year CAGR of -0.62%, far worse than the sector average.

Now, since the firm just exited their marinas segment last year and is now focusing on what I hope will be more optimal segments, 2026 should be critical in showing investors that this strategic direction proved to be right, and the next earnings results ought to provide some language in that regard, which I'll be looking for.

Until then, I gave this REIT a neutral/hold in this category.

Balance Sheet Risk

In terms of balance sheet risk, a key upside metric for this REIT is having a D/E of just 0.61, lower than its two key peers, but also showing a declining trend in the last year, a good sign of lower leverage:

Besides a low and improving D/E, let's not ignore that according to the company investor deck (pg 13), we can see this REIT has some strong credit ratings, including a BBB+ from S&P:

I think this can move the risk needle favorably, as opposed to a REIT that is not rated at all or has a below-investment-grade rating and serious balance sheet concerns. It can also help get more favorable funding in the future in a sector that is highly impacted by the cost of capital and interest rate movements.

As someone who has been writing about REITs for over a year now and has also been interested in the topic of risk management for investors, it is worth pointing out that this firm's largest asset exposure is to a non-liquid asset, real estate. In fact, balance sheet data shows it held $10.5B in net real estate assets at the quarter ending September.

While these income-producing assets can also be used for depreciation that helps lower reported earnings (a common topic among equity REITs), they are also assets that cannot be liquidated as easily as tradeable investment securities or cash, the most liquid of assets, since liquidating real estate requires waiting on an able and willing buyer.

So, for this reason, I think REITs like this are heavily dependent on cash flow from the properties themselves as opposed to an insurance company or bank, which can often raise cash by selling securities held (such as Treasuries). This is an important distinction when thinking about long-term risk as a REIT investor. With that said, the concern is not only the lumpy FFO trends I mentioned earlier but also that operating cash flow declined -1.25% YoY.

Therefore, my view is down the middle when it comes to this topic, and I gave the stock a hold in this category. What could move the needle up is the next few quarters showing improved cashflow and FFO trends from all that real estate.

Dividend Outlook

As I often talk about REITs as dividend income opportunities, this one is no different, but let's break down what some of those metrics could mean for dividend investors.

The numbers that you should care about are AFFO per share (income statement, quarter ending September), showing $2.18, while the quarterly dividend is $1.04. This implies around a 48% payout ratio. (quarterly dividend/quarterly AFFO).

Based on my benchmark of 75% or less, this is an attractive payout ratio, leaving lots of cash wiggle room after dividends are paid.

This REIT also has proven itself as a dividend grower, despite the lumpy FFO trends, with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +4.5%, beating the sector average.

We can visualize that in the chart below from Seeking Alpha:

Notice that the REIT did not make a dividend cut during the pandemic years 2020-2021, as some hotel REITs I've studied had to do, and also the market shock of higher interest rates in the last few years, which drives up the cost of borrowing in real estate. This is a sign of long-term resilience in the face of shocks and a lumpy FFO/cash flow trend.

The dividend yield of 3.2% is nothing too remarkable to go into in more detail, since there are some REITs well above that yield, so I think the focus here should be on sustainability and growth.

I am convinced it is a buy in this category, and the evidence above supports it.

Valuation Metrics

In this topic, let's consider the following worksheet I made, which compares valuation to potential price upside for this stock:

Using FFO consensus data for Dec. 2026 and valuation data on Seeking Alpha, this worksheet shows this stock is relatively in line with peers when it comes to its forward earnings multiple, while it is considerably undervalued on its price-to-book multiple. I believe this could indicate the market is expecting it to perform relatively in line with peers and the future earnings of this niche but is not paying a premium for this firm's asset portfolio.

Earlier, I mentioned how this REIT is exposed to illiquid real estate assets that seem to generate lumpy cash flow trends and seasonal impacts, so I can see why the market would pay a lower premium on this company's book, and also not a huge premium on future earnings (given the choppy trends).

My price target implies a 5% further upside, which is modest enough to justify a buy in this category, which should help the overall score as well.

Technical Charting

Now that we've covered many fundamental analysis topics that I think are relevant, as I've been doing lately in 2026, I'm including some technical charting to add additional angles to this story, something I did not do in my original article:

In this example where I compare the share price over 1 year vs. the 50-day and 200-day simple moving average/SMA, here is what comes to mind for me.

There were several buy signals (golden crosses) where the 50-day crossed above the 200-day average, but they were also followed by sell signals (death crosses) where it dipped below the 200-day again. Right now, the two moving averages are really tight together.

Because of no clear buy or sell signal at the moment, I went with a hold in this category, believing a watch-and-wait could be a good angle to take here until a clear breakout is evident.

This view did not meaningfully change the overall score from being bullish, however.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

A key element of my bullish view is supported by the low supply of mobile/manufactured homes compared to demand, which favors Sun Communities. However, I think a challenge to this view is that in the near future certain megacap players could move into this space and scoop up portfolios of existing MH properties or even buy out one of Sun's peers like ELS or UMH, in part or in full, thereby putting the squeeze on Sun's ability to organically grow its portfolio at a reasonable cost.

I think large players like Blackstone or others could increasingly recognize the demand potential in this niche, and increasingly deploy capital here. In fact, data from a private equity watchdog organization shows that names like Blackstone, Brookfield, and Apollo have already been investing in manufactured home properties, so I think investors should consider what moves such firms could make in this niche in the future.

Conclusion

Today's follow-up article, which agrees with the bullish view of Wall Street, upgraded Sun Communities to a buy this time, and key upside drivers overcame some challenges mentioned, with the most prominent upside potential coming from the low supply of mobile homes vs. the demand potential in an economy where housing affordability as well as lifestyle trend changes have an impact on where people move to and live.

In upcoming earnings results, the key points that I think could move the needle are whether the upgraded revenue guidance will be met or exceeded, as well as evidence of further portfolio growth in the MH and RV niche, in the wake of the firm exiting the marinas business.

Rating Methodology

Insights by Albert Anthony is the author's research approach that aims to tell a holistic story about a stock, starting with the larger macro/sector overview and narrowing down to a detailed technical price trend, focused on concisely answering questions like what could move the stock in the future, what is its market advantage, and does its valuation make sense in relation to future upside potential?

data.title: A Weak Dollar And Musk's Excellence Make Tesla Undervalued Today (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A weaker USD increasingly seems to be a direct objective of US economic policy, rather than a byproduct of it. President Trump recently said he is not worried about the USD weakening and, on the contrary, he thinks the value of the dollar is "great."

As a Swiss-based investor, I am painfully aware of the decline in value of the USD (USD:CHF). My portfolio has declined by almost 5% Year-To-Date in Swiss Francs. This is despite my performance in USD being positive.

Today, I want to cover the reasons why a weaker dollar may be an objective of this administration, as well as why this may benefit Tesla, Inc (TSLA), which I see as the only Tech manufacturing company in the West.

Why a weaker dollar is an accelerator for US manufacturing and exports

A weaker U.S. dollar should theoretically benefit the current U.S. administration in the following ways:

  1. It should increase U.S. exports and lower the U.S. trade deficit, as a direct result of American goods becoming cheaper and therefore more attractive on global markets.

  2. It should increase the attractiveness of America as a manufacturing destination, again a result of USD CapEx being more affordable for companies with a global footprint.

  3. It should also create higher nominal GDP growth and push the nominal price of USD assets higher, as a mechanical effect of net exports (a part of GDP) increasing and improving companies’ earnings.

Of course, not all is good. A weaker U.S. dollar could also represent a headwind for inflation, as imports get more expensive for Americans. This in turn may threaten the current (somewhat) dovish direction of the Federal Reserve. The Fed sets interest rates based on maximum employment and inflation. A weak U.S. dollar could simultaneously increase employment (via manufacturing jobs and investments in America) as well as create inflation (both as a result of more people employed and imports being more expensive).

Additionally, the protectionist direction of the current U.S. administration in matters of global trade may work against the intended goal of supporting U.S. exports. A less globalized world is a world where exports are more difficult as a result of tariffs and other trade barriers. While this has not yet manifested, with current U.S. trade agreements being generally favorable to America, it is a risk to keep in mind.

To be clear, my intent today is not to discuss the merits of “Trumpnomics,” which I covered in the past. In the context of Tesla, what matters of the above is that America is becoming a cheaper place to create stuff. And Tesla, in my view, happens to be the company that is working on the most exciting stuff.

Enter Tesla, the only Tech manufacturer in the West

In my past coverage of Tesla I have discussed at length about CEO Elon Musk’s vision for a fully automated world. The infographic below, which I used in my past work, details the areas where Musk’s companies play in that context. At the time of writing, Tesla is the one company with the broadest scope in Musk’s vision and the only publicly listed company (even if others are rumored to be in the future).

Beyond its role in Musk’s own ecosystem, I see Tesla as the only Western-based Tech company that is heavily investing in a large footprint of directly owned and operated manufacturing capabilities.

Companies like Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), Apple, Inc (AAPL) and Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) own the intellectual property of the most advanced microchips in the world. However, these chips are manufactured by contractors such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), often outside of the U.S. In the case of Apple, the company doesn’t operate its own factories despite selling its own software prevalently on its own hardware (be it iPhones, Macbooks or other devices).

Companies like Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) are undisputed leaders in software. I am heavily exposed to these names myself, but they are not manufacturing companies by any stretch of the imagination.

The only Tech companies with a significant manufacturing footprint that come to my mind are:

  • Intel (INTC), which operates multiple fabs in the USA but has lost its edge in microchip production to the likes of Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), which also has no directly operated fab.

  • ASML Holding N.V. (ASML), a Dutch company and a rare example of Tech innovation from outside of the U.S.

  • Smaller players in the microchip industry such as Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON) and Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), operating their own U.S.-based fabs and factories.

All these Tech companies, however, have manufacturing capabilities that are narrowly dedicated to the semiconductor industry. In simple words, none of these companies would be able to go from producing chips to producing robots or cars.

Tesla is the only Western Tech company, in my view, that is actively building such capabilities. It currently produces EV cars, which are arguably the most difficult type of Tech manufacturing. It also plans to build humanoid robots, which Musk expects to be rolled out to consumers in 2027. And the company is also planning a fab to produce its own microchips.

To the above, one has to add the manufacturing capabilities of companies in Musk’s ecosystem. SpaceX (SPACE) is to date one of the few companies that can produce commercial-grade spacecraft. xAI is also planning their own microchips.

To find a direct competitor to Tesla, one needs to look at China. Only there, conglomerates like Xiaomi Corporation (XIACF) are following Tesla’s playbook.

What does it all mean in the context of a weak U.S. dollar?

I think a weaker dollar represents a strong tailwind for Tesla’s manufacturing ambitions in two ways.

First and most importantly, it makes investing in America cheaper. And the company has a lot of CapEx planned for the next five years.

Tesla’s 2025 CapEx consisted of around ~$8.5 Billion. 2026 CapEx is expected to "increase substantially" from 2025 levels to support growth in AI initiatives (e.g., AI5 chips), autonomy (e.g., Cybercab), robotics (e.g., Optimus), vehicle/energy expansions and data centers. While Tesla did not provide precise guidance on CapEx in its newly released earnings, its ambitions clearly need more manufacturing capacity and CapEx investment.

The second effect of a weaker dollar is making (part) of Tesla’s exports more attractive on global markets. To be fair, the company doesn’t only produce in the U.S., with significant manufacturing capabilities in China and Europe. Around ~40% of global vehicle production output comes from China exclusively.

This will inevitably reduce the “tailwind effect” of a weaker U.S. dollar, but the future footprint of Tesla’s production, especially that not related to cars, may look more U.S.-centric and therefore more attractive pricing-wise.

Additionally, China is increasingly isolated from the U.S. in terms of trade (meaning the Chinese production can be absorbed within China) and the company reports earnings in USD. Both factors should help Tesla in this context.

The market may soon rotate to Tesla and other risky assets

What happens when trust in a fiat currency rapidly erodes? I have discussed this topic in detail in my past coverage, arguing that U.S.-based investors should simply stop reasoning in USD terms. Based on historical parallelisms and macroeconomics fundamentals, asset prices always skyrocket in nominal terms in context of high-inflation and erosion in government trust.

Capital fleeing towards risk assets during periods of low rates and lack of confidence in fiat currency is a well known phenomenon. In fact, I think Trump is only re-starting and accelerating a long term trend, where assets grew in nominal value mostly due to the debasement of currencies, and in particular the U.S. dollar.

During the era of ZIRP (Zero-Interest-Rate-Policy) and Quantitative Easing we already saw risky assets skyrocketing. Venture Capital investments reached historical highs, and the Nasdaq-100 went on a multi-year bull run.

The ZIRP era also corresponded to Tesla’s historical bull run, with the stock (split-adjusted) surging from the mid-20s to over $235 per share by the end of 2020. That, of course, did not happen only as a function of increased liquidity. Tesla was able to attract capitals given its ability in scaling up production of Model 3s and Model Ys, showing it could bring its vision of mobility to reality.

In this sense, I believe Tesla could once again attract capital in a context of weak dollar and low rates, given the executional excellence it displayed in Q4 earnings, which I will cover next.

Q4 earnings show we may be close to a new “Model 3 moment”

I see Q4 2025 earnings as pivotal for Tesla. I think Q4 showed how Tesla is really switching from being a car-centric robotics company to a full-fledged robotics company. In this sense, I believe we may be in a similar situation to where Tesla was before the company fully scaled their Model 3 and Model Y production, becoming a mainstream carmaker. This makes me see asymmetric potential, to the point that I decided to upgrade my rating from Buy to Strong Buy.

There are many elements in Q4 earnings that make me say Musk is displaying executional excellence in his vision towards a world of “amazing abundance”.

  • Tesla energy storage growing at +49% YoY.

  • Active FSD (Full Self-Drive) +38% reaching 1.1 million customers.

  • Model X and Model S production coming to an end to free up production of Optimus robots.

  • New roll-out dates announced for Robotaxi coverage in the U.S. (see image below).

  • Tesla’s $2 Billion investment in xAI, which signals how Tesla is closely integrated in Musk’s overall ecosystem.

All the elements above have one thing in common: they are not about producing cars to sell to consumers. And that’s because Tesla is not a car company, but an AI robotics company and the only Tech manufacturing giant in the West.

What is especially significant to me is the fact that Tesla is dropping production of their high-end Model S and Model X. I think this makes a lot of sense. The Model S is a niche high performance sedan car. The Model X is a large SUV that is going to be rendered obsolete by the Model YL, a longer variation that went to market in China recently. Both Models represent only a fraction of Tesla's production capacity (see below). I think giving up on these in favor of Optimus is a testament to Tesla's vision.

In this context, I see a parallelism to where Tesla was in 2014-2015, before rolling out Model 3 and Model Y to the masses. In Tesla’s own words:

Our approach to autonomous vehicles and humanoid robots mirrors the way we approached electric vehicles and energy storage - at the system level where we identify the limiting factor and develop bespoke and scalable solutions (batteries, power electronics, inverters, software, AI silicon, etc.) to optimize for cost, functionality, efficiency and safety.

If Tesla manages to execute on its vision - a big if - we may be 1 to 2 years away from a new “Model 3 moment” with Optimus and Robotaxis scaling up. The difference this time is that Tesla won’t be competing on price with a plethora of legacy carmakers.

For robotics, there won’t be any real competition from other Western companies. There will be competition from Chinese players (which are currently almost denied access from the American market due to tariffs and trade tensions) and other robotics companies based in Asia, for example in South Korea.

For robotaxis, it will compete with the likes of Waymo and UBER, with the difference that it will be the only company to be vertically integrated from car production to software and consumer-facing App.

That’s why I am upgrading Tesla to a Strong Buy. It is a high-risk bet on the chance Tesla grows to be a quasi-monopolist in robotics in the West. These last earnings gave me enough reassurance that I can now see Musk’s vision having a chance of becoming a reality. If this happens, I see a potential of up to ~6X current share prices (more of my thoughts in the Conclusion section), making this an asymmetric bet.

Risks: if you think Tesla should be valued based on its financials, look elsewhere

Based on its financial metrics, Tesla’s valuation is simply insane. This is why I invite readers that are convinced stocks should price past earnings to avoid this company. They may be better off considering tobacco companies, which still see attractive valuations and low P/E numbers. Alternatively, U.S.-based skeptics may want to simply hold cash in USD and T-Bills, which can still yield ~4-5% per year. As long as they do not plan to frequently vacation abroad, I think they should be safely covered for inflation. They can then opt to deploy this cash in the next 80%+ market crash.

Only readers that are open to the idea of a new currency debasement-fueled rotation towards risky assets may want to consider Tesla. However, even those readers should consider that Tesla’s plans are not guaranteed by any stretch of the imagination. Asymmetric returns do not come without high risk, and Tesla is no exception. Many things could go wrong. Just to list a few:

  • Optimus roll-out may be slowed down by technical difficulties in production.

  • There may not be enough business and consumer demand for humanoid robots.

  • Robotaxis could face regulatory hurdles, especially outside the U.S.

  • Elon Musk could become too political and alienate Tesla’s customer base further.

Ultimately, a world of abundance may never manifest. It may be that humanity will always stay around the technological developments in cars, robotics and AI that we see today, in January 2026.

Other risks include volatility of the stock and shareholders’ dilution, given the heavy CapEx investment needs Tesla is facing. Finally, from a macro perspective, interest rates may go down slower than what the market anticipates and the USD may strengthen if policymaking changes.

Conclusion and my price target for Tesla

Many investors, especially international ones, are currently considering reducing exposure to U.S. assets due to the forex risk connected to the U.S. dollar. I find this approach nonsensical. As an investor, I will always give priority to the underlying assets I invest in. I will always prefer excellent, innovative companies such as Tesla to non-U.S. laggards.

That’s why I hold Tesla, which I see as the only Tech manufacturer in the West. It only helps that a weaker U.S. dollar, which the current U.S. admin seems to be pursuing, is set to help Tesla. The company plans to invest billions in CapEx in the next few years to bring its vision of full automation to reality.

In terms of price target, my followers know I refrain from giving exact targets, as my single stock picks are always selected with a long term investment horizon in mind. This said, in line with my previous work, I think that Tesla could easily reach a $10 Trillion valuation if it executes on its vision.

The table below, taken from this previous article of mine, outlines a scenario of the growth needed for Tesla to grow into a $10 Trillion giant. Data refers to Q2 2025.

A $10 Trillion valuation would represent a share price of roughly $3,000 per share. This is my directional target for this high risk asymmetric bet, and the reason why I recommend a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon: Buy Ahead Of Q4 Earnings (Preview)
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) doesn't require a long introduction. It's one of the biggest companies in the world, which doesn't lose its growth. AMZN went from $107 billion in sales in 2025 to over $637 billion in sales as of TTM, based on Seeking Alpha, and the operating income went from about $2.2 billion to ~$68.6 billion for the same period. What's noteworthy, the forward estimates don't indicate any major slowdown, at least over the next few years, making such a large company unique for the level of its continued expansion.

I think the stock looks quite attractive, although the stock price is close to its all-time highs. The company is set to report its Q4 results on February 5th, which is in about a week from now, and I see quite high odds for another "blowout" quarter, as analysts say. And it's not just gut feeling or a nod to the past few quarters. Recent events make current consensus expectations quite pessimistic and fragile, especially for what's coming beyond Q4. FY2026 guidance should be strong, in my opinion, and it’s going to provide a nice catalyst for bullish momentum.

AMZN is a "Buy" ahead of its upcoming earnings report.

Let's Assess The Expectations For AMZN's Q4

Amazon reported for its Q3 in late October, and despite seeing consensus estimates that priced a premium over the management's guidance, the firm was able to beat the estimates on both revenue and EPS. And the beats were quite strong - 1.35% and 25.3%, respectively, based on Seeking Alpha. In general, the Q3 EPS beat was the 11th in a row, and despite that 25%+ already looking quite solid, it looks like a common thing for AMZN (the weakest beat since late 2022 was 16.77%).

The management set a revenue guidance for Q4 at $206-213 billion, implying a YoY growth rate at 11.56%. The late-October consensus was expecting ~$207.95 billion, according to Seeking Alpha, so it was closer to the lower end of the range. As a result, knowing Amazon's tendency to usually give conservative guidance, the analysts rushed to update their model and put their own premium to the new midpoint. Naturally, this process touched the EPS side simultaneously - hence the abundance of bullish earnings revisions for Q4 that we saw right after the Q3 earnings commentaries:

The track record is on the side of Amazon ahead of its Q4 release, but it's not the only reason I expect another double-beating from the company.

You might have watched or heard of the recently held World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. I particularly liked Jensen Huang's speech, which was dedicated to AI infrastructure and everything tied to it. Nvidia's (NVDA) CEO defined the interconnected structure of energy, chips/computing infrastructure, cloud, AI models, and the application layer as a "five-layer cake". Everything underneath this structure is essential - especially the infrastructure.

We're now a few hundred billion dollars into it. We're a few hundred billion dollars into it. Larry and I, we get the opportunity to work on many projects together. There are trillions of dollars of infrastructure that needs to be built out. And it's sensible. It's sensible because all of these contexts have to be processed so that the AI, so that the models can generate the intelligence necessary to power the applications that ultimately sit on top.

Seeking Alpha, link above

Amazon stands out as the key player with a very high probability of success from its FY2025 CAPEX plan, which has already been executed, and FY2026 CAPEX should exceed $150 billion (the highest among the "Magnificent 7"). In Q4 in particular, AMZN is going to add another gigawatt (and even more) on the path of doubling the overall capacity by the end of 2027.

On the capacity side, we brought in quite a bit of capacity, as I mentioned in my opening comments, 3.8 gigawatts of capacity in the last year with another gigawatt plus coming in the fourth quarter, and we expect to double our overall capacity by the end of 2027.

AMZN's Q3 earnings call

The addition of that capacity in Q4 should help Amazon address the sold out AWS environment where demand for AI infrastructure currently outstrips available supply, based on the last quarter's commentaries. With that capacity, the conversion of backlog into real revenues should accelerate, and AWS revenues should maintain a high-margin profile compared to e-commerce margins. As a result, the shift toward AWS - and the support of existing operating leverage - appears to be here to stay, making a beat toward the high end of both revenue and EPS consensus estimates more likely, in my opinion.

When it comes to the capacity built-out beyond Q4, the EPS growth opportunity looks even clearer because they're basically positioning their most profitable segment to deliver the outsized operating income over the next few years. And that's why the downward revisions on FY2027 EPS figures that we saw look too aggressive to me. I think the market will gradually realize that this negativity isn't supported by Amazon's factual execution.

From that, I should note that the fear over FCF deterioration that we've been seeing looks overblown because of that - the future ROIC should come from the backlog monetization as the capacity scales up. Once the market realizes this, I'd expect a repricing to the upside.

Another bullish thing that should influence the post-Q4 price action is the management's latest decision to lay off roughly 10% of its corporate workforce, switching from high-cost labor to AI-driven agents and machine learning models.

Effectively, they can save on the FCF spent and redirect these payroll savings into its AI infrastructure development. And the margins might go up on an adjusted basis, so I'd expect a boost to 2026-2027 EPS revisions.

Amazon Is One Of The Cheapest Among Mega Caps

When we look at the 1-year forward price-to-earnings multiples among mega-cap firms, we'll notice that Amazon is priced close to or below the average of the peer group.

But compared to everyone else on the list, AMZN is going to expand its EPS (in two years from now) by the highest growth rate.

I think the growth superiority to similar-sized peers gives AMZN a moral right to trade at 28-30x earnings by the end of 2027.

The company beat the analysts' annual consensus EPS by 6.8% and 7.46% for FY2023 and FY2024, respectively, so I expect at least an average of it to FY2026, and then 5% to FY2027 EPS consensus.

Consensus EPS FY2026 * 1.0713 = forecasted FY2026 EPS

Forecasted FY2027 EPS = forecasted FY2026 EPS * consensus growth rate * 1.05

As a result, I get the following FY2027 EPS expectation:

$7.87 * 1.0713 = $8.431131

Forecasted FY2027 EPS = $8.431131 * 1.2185 * 1.05 = $10.79

At 29x earnings, my price target for Amazon over the next 12 months arrives at $312.82 per share, which is 28.7% above today's stock price.

Risks To My Views

The biggest risk to my views comes from the timing of my call. The stock may dip even amid a strong earnings release just like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) stock did recently. The market might be right about the lack of ROIC on Amazon's AI CAPEX, and a lot of the FCF deterioration we saw may have come with no added value. In addition to that, the upcoming Q4 data, or the next few quarters, might show us Amazon's declining competitive position against rivals like Azure or GCP, which seem to be growing more rapidly, as of late 2025. Also, the Q3 net income was inflated by a $9.5 billion gain from the revaluation of Amazon's investment in Anthropic, which may not add anything to the bottom line in Q4.

Conclusion

I like AMZN at its current stock price. Right before the earnings call, the stock looks quite inexpensive for the level of the business growth that should come in the next few years. I see an upside potential of over 28% in the next 12 months, and I expect a very strong Q4 print next week.

data.title: Interim Data Spotlight: Taiwan Semiconductor, Tesla, Costco
data.description:
Macroeconomic volatility feels like it has been ratcheted up a few notches in recent years. Stock prices, interest rates, commodity trends, and currency pairs turn on a dime, while official economic data is harder to collect and interpret. Increasingly, company-specific fundamental data is sought after for breadcrumbs on the broader backdrop.

Wall Street Horizon’s industry-leading interim data calendar helps portfolio managers, institutional investors, and traders map what’s happening at the firm level to the global economy.

Dig Deeper with Interim Data

But what is interim data? It’s company-released performance information shared between regular earnings reports. More plainly, interim data is official, scheduled updates that give investors a peek at how a company is doing before the next quarterly or semi-annual earnings release.

What Counts as Interim Data?

Interim data typically includes:

  • Intra-period financial statements (common outside North America)

  • Sales updates (monthly or quarterly)

  • Production figures (e.g., autos produced, tons mined, barrels pumped)

  • Operating metrics (passenger volumes, same-store sales, service levels)

  • Pre-earnings trading updates or performance snapshots

These releases are company-initiated, time-bound, scheduled, and quantitative-heavy. Impactful enough to move single stocks and industry indexes, there’s less CEO “fluff” with interim data. It can be ideal for money managers and analysts preferring hard facts, not soft narratives.

Going Beyond Earnings Reports

Quarterly updates from U.S.-based multinational corporations come in fast and furious beginning in the middle of January, April, July, and October. International firms, by contrast, commonly post revenue and profit numbers semi-annually. This cadence is helpful from a scheduling perspective—knowing when volatility may come about—but it naturally results in gaps on the calendar.

Interim data fills the void. Distanced from earnings season, periodic fundamental updates (often monthly) allow for more time to digest the data and interpret what it means at the micro and macro scale.

Taiwan Semiconductor: Eyeing Interim Data for Tells on the AI Trade

For instance, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) releases a revenue report normally on the 10th of each month. Some pundits assert that the world’s most valuable publicly traded non-U.S. company is the most crucial to the modern financial market. The chipmaker’s market cap is near $1.5 trillion, and eyes are constantly on tensions between Taiwan and China. Investors can play the geopolitical guessing game all they want, but perhaps the better tack is to analyze actual sales trends—imagine that!

As of early 2025, TSM appears to be firing on all cylinders. Unfortunately, shareholders and market participants with ties to the AI trade and semiconductors will have to wait until mid-April for its next quarterly earnings report. Savvy investors tracking interim data have the upper hand—TSM’s January and February monthly revenue reports will offer first glimpses of what the Street will uncover months down the road.

TSM’s January 2026 revenue report hits on February 10, with the following update confirmed for March 10.

Tesla: Quarterly First-Takes

From the most valuable non-U.S. company to the world’s richest person, Elon Musk’s Tesla (TSLA) stands out as the most volatile stock in the Magnificent Seven. Leading into earnings events over the last few years, options pricing has averaged about an 8% implied share price swing. From 2023 to 2025, the mean actual move was 9.7%, with a range of -12% to +22%. With volatile stocks, having the most up-to-date data is critical to managing risk.

Tesla reports production and delivery figures within days of quarter-end, making it an ideal time to closely parse the numbers (as earnings season on Wall Street heats up just two weeks thereafter). What’s more, the EV maker’s full quarterly report is published about three weeks after the interim data. What Musk and other executives detail on the earnings conference call often stems directly from the interim data.

Tesla issues Q4 2025 earnings after market today, Wednesday, January 28, while the Q1 2026 event is slated for Tuesday, April 21. The next set of interim data should cross the wires on Thursday, April 2. Ahead of tonight’s main event, the options market prices in an unusually low 5.8% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the Q4 report, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services.

Of course, monthly interim production data from the likes of Ford (F), General Motors (GM), and European and Asian automakers provide more pure-play vehicle reads, which help with gauging the cyclical slice of the global economy.

Costco: Consumer Clues & the K-Shaped Economy

Finally, consumer trends can shift quickly. Yes, the monthly Retail Sales report put out by the U.S. Census Bureau is somewhat timely and broad-based. About two weeks before its release, though, Costco (COST) produces its monthly same-store sales report. When the numbers drop (usually on the first Wednesday or Thursday afternoon after the first of the month), the Consumer Staples sector pays attention.

Right now, the “K-shaped” economy is all the buzz. Costco’s interim data homes in on the upper dash of the K, given its middle- to high-income customer base. Recent reports have been mixed, suggesting that there could be shifts in the overall K.

We’ll find out more on February 4, March 5, and April 8.

The Bottom Line

Interim data gives investors an early signal on company performance before official earnings are released, often reshaping expectations and driving market volatility. Because these updates occur on a known schedule, they offer an opportunity to anticipate risk, refine forecasts, and position portfolios ahead of other price-moving events.

In a market where timing and information edge matter, interim announcements can be just as influential as earnings themselves.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
